coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite and its application as a photocatalyst for the degradation of an anionic dye, ARS
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Sharanjit
Sharma, Shelja
Kansal, Sushil Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-modified ZnS nanocomposite was prepared via a fast and facile chemical precipitation technique and was employed for the first time as a photocatalyst for the degradation of Alizarin red S (ARS) dye under visible light irradiation. The structural, morphological and optical properties of the prepared ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite were characterized by multiple analytical techniques such as X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis, photoluminescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, field emission scanning electron microscope equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. Impact of affecting parameters on the photocatalytic activity of the ZnS/CQDs was studied and optimized. The results showed that the ZnS/CQDs exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of ARS dye i.e. 89% within 250 min, higher than that of the bare ZnS (63%). This enhancement in photocatalytic activity of ZnS/CQDs was attributed to the introduction of CQDs, which could absorb visible light efficiently, suppressing the recombination of electron–hole pairs and improving charge separation. Moreover, various scavengers have been used to study the role of reactive species in the photocatalytic degradation process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: ZnS
Semiconductor
Carbon dots
Photocatalysis
ARS
Degradation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Investigation of the enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Typical XRD pattern of ZnS/CQDS nanocomposite.
(a, b) TEM images (c, d) FESEM images of ZnS/CQDs at different magnifications.
(a) FTIR spectrum (b) TGA of ZNS/CQDs nanocomposite.
XPS spectra of ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite for (a) Full spectra; (b) Zn 2p; (c) S 2p (d) O 1s; (e) C 1s; (e) Typical EDS spectrum.
(a) Room temperature photoluminescence spectra of nanocomposite at excitation wavelength of 315 nm (b) UV–vis DRS spectrum of ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite.
UV–vis absorption spectra of ARS dye at different pH. Inset shows the chemical structure of ARS dye.

(a) Time dependent UV–vis absorption spectra of ARS dye in the presence of ZnS/CQDs. (b) Comparison of photolysis and photocatalytic activity of bare ZnS and ZnS/CQDs (catalyst dose 0.5 g/L, dye concentration 10 mg/L, pH 4). (c) Degradation rate (%) of ARS dye in the presence of ZnS/CQDs at different pH (catalyst dose 0.5 g/L, dye concentration 10 mg/L). (d) Degradation rate (%) of ARS dye in the presence of ZnS/CQDs at different catalyst dose (dye concentration 10 mg/L, pH 4).
Effect of different scavengers on the degradation of ARS dye with ZnS/CQDs photocatalyst.
Diagrammatic representation of the separation and migration of photogenerated charge carriers in the Zns/CQDs with the reaction mechanism of photocatalytic process.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite and its application as a photocatalyst for the degradation of an anionic dye, ARS

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Dr. S.S. B. University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Dr. S.S. B. University Institute of Chemical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Centre of Advanced Studies, Panjab University, Chandigarh 160014, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs)-modified ZnS nanocomposite was prepared via a fast and facile chemical precipitation technique and was employed for the first time as a photocatalyst for the degradation of Alizarin red S (ARS) dye under visible light irradiation. The structural, morphological and optical properties of the prepared ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite were characterized by multiple analytical techniques such as X-ray diffraction, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, thermogravimetric analysis, photoluminescence spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, field emission scanning electron microscope equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy. Impact of affecting parameters on the photocatalytic activity of the ZnS/CQDs was studied and optimized. The results showed that the ZnS/CQDs exhibited excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of ARS dye i.e. 89% within 250 min, higher than that of the bare ZnS (63%). This enhancement in photocatalytic activity of ZnS/CQDs was attributed to the introduction of CQDs, which could absorb visible light efficiently, suppressing the recombination of electron–hole pairs and improving charge separation. Moreover, various scavengers have been used to study the role of reactive species in the photocatalytic degradation process.

ce:section.ce:para: The breakthrough towards the evolution for designing competent photocatalysts is an integral matter of concern for the environmental remediation issues because of production of large scale organic pollutants from the textile and food industries. Varieties of dye are produced in enormous quantities all over the world to meet the requirements of textile industry. The Alizarin red S (ARS) is an anionic dye which is widely used in woven fabrics, wool and cotton textiles [1,2]. When untreated wastewater containing these types of dyes is thrown into the environment, lots of health problems are being faced by living organisms. Therefore, it becomes one of the challenging tasks to treat the wastewater containing dyes.

Heterogeneous photocatalysis has been emerged as a promising technology which can treat wastewater effectively. Till now, numerous oxides and sulfides semiconductor nanomaterials have been synthesized, researched and utilized as photocatalysts in this process [3–10]. In particular, zinc sulfide (ZnS) is flourishing nowadays as of being its high photocatalytic activity, cost effectiveness and unique band structure. Due to generation of electron and hole pairs upon photoexcitation and highly negative reduction potential of electrons makes ZnS a good photocatalytic material [11,12]. However, a rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers and the confined absorption band of the ZnS photocatalyst are the major limitations that restrict its applicability to the photocatalysis. Thus, a plenty of endeavors have been made to improve the photocatalytic behavior of ZnS. In order to overcome these issues, an appropriate modification is imperative for developing advanced varieties of ZnS nanostructures [13,14].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a novel issue and gained significant attention due to its numerous attributes and outstanding photophysical properties [15]. Particularly, its size and excitation wavelength-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior would improve the photocatalytic properties of the CQDs-based composites [16]. It was reported that the electron-transfer and accepting properties of CQDs present a favorable approach to obstruct the recombination of photogenerated electrons and holes; accordingly, enhanced photocatalytic performance can be accomplished by the fabrication of semiconductor/CQDs composites [17]. Lately, the construction of complex photocatalysts (CQDs/BiOI, WO3/CQDs, NZnO/CQDs) has exhibited considerable proficiency towards photocatalytic degradation of organic contaminants [18–20]. Martins et al. demonstrated the photocatalytic activity of nitrogen doped CQDs/TiO2 composite for photo-oxidation of NO under UV and visible light irradiation [21]. Qian et al. reported the synthesis of CQDSBi2WO6 for removing gaseous volatile organic compounds photocatalytically under UV and visible light irradiation [22]. In view of remarkable characteristic properties of CQDs and the drawbacks of ZnS photocatalytic system, the consolidation of CQDs and ZnS can be accounted as an exemplary strategy to design exceptionally efficient photocatalytic system.

In this work, we report a facile methodology for synthesizing ZnS, CQDs, and ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite. The results interpreted that the CQDs were successfully combined with the ZnS nanoparticles and the introduction of CQDs could effectively enhance the amount and the transfer ratio of the photogenerated charge carrier, which was the key factor for the enhanced photocatalytic activity for the degradation of ARS dye under visible light irradiation.

All the chemicals are commercially available and brought into use as received. Zinc acetate dihydrate (Zn(OAc)2.·2H2O), sodium dodecyl sulfate (CH3(CH2)11SO4Na), sodium azide (NaN3), potassium iodide (KI), formic acid (HCOOH) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were bought from Merck, India. ARS (C. I. No. 58005)(C14H6Na2O7S),a hydrophilic anthraquinone dye was purchased from SD fine Chemicals, India. Sodiumsulfide hydrate (Na2S. H2O) and l-ascorbic acid were procured from Himedia, India. All the stock solutions were prepared in distilled water.

A facile co-precipitation method was adopted for the synthesis of ZnS. In a typical reaction process, 25 mL of 0.5 M of Zn (OAc)2 2H2O was added into 25 mL of 0.5 M Na2S dropwise with vigorous stirring. 0.5 M SDS solution (25 mL) was added into above resultant solution under stirring. White precipitates tend to form; these were filtered and washed with ethanol and water several times. Then precipitates were dried in an oven at 60 °C for overnight.

For the synthesis of CQDs, 0.25 g of ascorbic acid was immersed in 50 mL of ethanol and 20 mL of water. The resultant solution was kept under stirring for 10 min. Then it was transferred to Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave and kept in oven for 3 h at 160 °C. Finally the brown alcoholic suspension of CQDs was obtained. 50 μL of alcoholic suspension of CQDs was again diluted with 50 mL of ethanol and 0.1 g of above ZnS was added into it. It was kept under stirring for an hour. The resultant precipitates were then washed with distilled water and filtered off and dried in an oven.

The structure and crystal phase of the as-prepared catalyst were analyzed withX-ray diffraction (XRD) using Proto Analytical X-ray Diffraction System (AXRD) fitted with a Cu Kα radiation source. The XRD patterns of all the samples were recorded in 10°–80° region with a 0.04 step size (Dwell time = 1 s). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were obtained with AMICUS, Kratos Analytical (Shimadzu), spectroscope. The binding energy in XPS analysis was corrected by the C (1s) adventitious peak. The general morphology of the synthesized nanoparticles was analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), using a TEM; JEOL-JEM-2100Fand Field emission transmission electron microscopy (FESEM), using HITACHI, SU8010equipped with energy dispersive spectroscope (EDS) at an accelerating voltage of 10 kV. The UV–vis absorption studies of the photocatalysts were conducted on UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2450) using BaSO4as a reference. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed by using a Thermoscientific (Nicolet iS50) FTIR spectrometer, equipped with a diamond ATR at room temperature in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The room temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured on a Hitachi F-7000 Fluorescence spectrophotometer (5J1-004 model)using quartz cuvette 1 cm path length at an excitation wavelength of 315 nm.

The photocatalytic behavior of as synthesized ZnS/CQDs was investigated by examining the degradation of aqueous solution of ARS dye (10 mg/L) under visible light (λ = 400–520 nm, Philips 150 W, 1475 Lux). The photocatalytic experiment was carried out in a specially designed double walled reactor. During the reaction, the temperature was maintained constant by circulating water in the reactor jacket. The pH conditions and catalyst dose were also varied to obtain the optimum process parameters. In order to study the photolytic reaction, control experiment was also carried out in the absence of catalyst. The dye solution along with catalyst was stirred in dark for 30 min for achieving adsorption and desorption equilibrium between catalyst and dye molecules. Then it was exposed to the visible light. The fixed amount of aliquots of dye solution was drawn out at different time intervals with the help of syringe and filtered through the Chromafil syringe filters of size 0.45 μm and inspected for the remaining dye concentration in the solution by obtaining the absorption spectra using Systronics-2202 spectrophotometer in the wavelength ranging from 200 to 800 nm. The degradation rate (%) was determined from the equation as mentioned below:
(1)
Degradation
rate
( % )
=
(
C 0
− C )
/
C 0
× 100
Where, C0 and C are the initial concentration and concentration of the dye after t (min) of the photocatalytic reaction under visible irradiations.

Also, different kinds of scavengers (KI, NaN3, HCOOH, NaCl) were used to investigate the role of reactive species in the photocatalytic process. The scavengers of concentration 0.01 M were introduced into the dye solution before the addition of catalyst.

The crystal structure and phase purity of ZnS/CQDs was investigated using XRD. As shown in Fig. 1 , the diffraction peaks obtained can be attributed to the crystal planes of (111), (220) and (311) respectively. All the obtained diffraction peaks are related to cubic phase of zinc blende structure (JCPDS card no. 05–0566). For CQDs modified ZnS, no characteristic peak of CQDs could be observed, which may be due to its quite lower content present in ZnS. The obtained XRD peaks are broadened, indicating that the size of particles is in nanometer range. No other diffraction peaks of impurities were observed suggesting the high purity of sample [23].

The general morphological studies of as-prepared sample were determined by TEM and FESEM. The typical TEM images (Fig. 2 (a) and (b)) demonstrate that prepared catalyst consists of irregular nanoparticles with average diameter less than 10 nm. From the FESEM images as shown in Fig. 2(c) and (d), it was concluded that the prepared sample was grown in high density and agglomerated.

The FTIR spectrum of ZnS/CQDs nanoparticles is recorded in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. In Fig. 3 (a), the peaks at 3200 cm−1and 1633 cm−1 are due to stretching vibrations of OH group, and bending vibrations of water molecules absorbed on ZnS/CQDs. The absorption band at 2915 cm−1,2845 cm−1and 960 cm−1attributes to C H stretching and rocking band vibrations, respectively. The bands centered at 1048 cm−1and 1250 cm−1might be ascribed to CC and CO stretching vibrations, respectively [24]. The peaks in the region from 500 cm−1 to 1000 cm−1 indicate the presence of metal sulfide bands [25].

Thermogravimetric analysis was performed to determine the total weight loss of the prepared catalyst with respect to temperature. It was found that there was total 33% weight loss with the temperature ranging from 0 to 1000 °C (Fig. 3(b)). In the first stage 20% weight loss at around 350° C was due to desorption of adsorbed moisture from the surface. In the second stage, another 13%weight loss from the region 350° C to 1000 °C was attributed to the decomposition of organic chemical moieties attached via covalent linkages to the surface [26].

XPS experiments were studied to examine the composition and chemical bonding states of the ZnS/CQDs. The binding energy in XPS analysis was corrected by the C 1s adventitious peak at 287.7 eV Fig. 4 (a) depicts the full wide scan spectrum of ZnS/CQDs, confirming the existence of Zn 2p, S 2p and O 1s and C 1s. The high resolution XPS spectrum of the Zn and S elements as illustrated in Fig. 4(b) and (c) exhibit peaks at 1022.8, 1046.1 and 161.8 eV which correspond to the Zn 2p3/2, Zn 2p1/2and S 2p3/2 peaks of ZnS [27]. The obvious right shifts and broadening of characteristic peaks of Zn 2p and S 2p indicates the existing interactions of ZnS with CQDs. In Fig. 4(d) and (e), the high resolution spectrum for O 1s and C 1s can be seen. From XPS analysis, it can be concluded that ZnS and CQDs have been successfully coupled with each other. The energy dispersive spectroscopic measurements are also in well agreement with the results of XPS studies. It can be observed that there are signals of zinc, sulfur, carbon and oxygen with varied elements percentage. The elemental composition of different elements in ZnS/CQDs is as shown in Table 1 given below:

The optical properties of ZnS/CQDs has been determined by photoluminescence and UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopic studies. The light absorption, charge migration and separation are the key characteristics for the high performance of ZnS/CQDs photocatalyst. Fig. 5 (a) shows the room temperature PL spectra of bare ZnS and ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite at an excitation wavelength of 315 nm. From the PL spectra, it was observed that bare ZnS nanoparticles exhibited two emission peaks at 408 nm and 432 nm [28]. These emission peaks in blue region have been acknowledged to the recombination of the electrons between the sulfur-vacancy-related donor and the valence band. Usually for semiconductor nanocrystals, two emission peaks are observed which can be corresponded to interstitial and the trapped surface states emission, respectively. But in ZnS/CQDs, the intensity of the emission band decreases significantly when the CQDs is introduced onto the ZnS surface as compared to the bare ZnS, implying that the ZnS/CQDs could effectively decrease the recombination of the photogenerated electrons and holes pairs [21]. As shown in Fig. 5(b), ZnS/CQDs exhibited strong absorption in the region from 200 nm to 800 nm. This visible light absorption is owed to the internal band gap transition. The absorption threshold of the prepared catalyst was calculated from UV–vis DRS curve. It was found that ZnS/CQDs showed steep absorption edge at λ = 412 nm. The intrinsic band gap energy can be determined from the equation given below:
(2)
E g
= hc / λeV = 1240 / λeV
Where Eg is the band gap, h is planck's constant and λ is the wavelength at absorption edge. Hence, the band gap of ZnS/CQDs was calculated to be 3 eV. It can be concluded that the as prepared catalyst can be utilized for visible/solar light induced catalyzed reactions.

The photocatalytic activity of as synthesized ZnS/CQDs was evaluated by the photocatalytic degradation of ARS dye under visible light. ARS dye is derived from alizarin by the introduction of sulfonate group in the alizarine structure. ARS dye is a pH sensitive dye. The UV–visible spectra of ARS is acquired according to different pH in Fig. 6 . In acidic medium, the color of the dye is pale yellow at pH 2 (λ max = 424 nm), which turns to yellow at pH 4 (λ max = 424 nm) and then to rose pink at pH 6 (λ max = 520 nm). This phenomenon occurs due to the disassociation of ARS at the two phenol groups, resulting in the formation of dianionic form. Also, the absorption band located at 260 nm is ascribed to π- π∗ transition of quinonic structure. From acidic to basic medium, the absorption intensities at 424 nm decreases and a new peak emerges at 520 nm [29].

Fig. 7 (a) shows the absorbance spectra of ARS dye in the presence of ZnS/CQDs at different time intervals under visible light. During the course of photocatalytic reaction, it was observed that the characteristic absorbance peak of ARS dye at 520 nm decreased gradually and got diminished in 4 h. About 89% of the ARS dye aqueous solution degraded within 250 min, higher than that of bare ZnS (63%) under similar conditions. It can be observed that no appreciable degradation of dye solution took place under visible light alone as well as in the presence of catalyst only (Fig. 7(b)). Hence, the photolytic phenomenon is not efficient for the degradation of ARS. The photocatalytic degradation of ARS dye was also monitored at different pH of dye solution keeping catalyst dose 0.5 g/L and dye concentration 10 mg/L constant. It is clear from Fig. 7(c), that by varying the pH of aqueous dye solution from 2 to 4, there is a significant difference in the photocatalytic activity of ZnS/CQDs. At pH 2, the observed photocatalytic degradation was 7.2% only, whereas at pH 4, the photocatalytic activity of ZnS/CQDs increases enormously up to 89%, which further decreases to 76.28% after changing the pH to 6, indicating the optimum value of pH 4. The optimum catalyst dose was also estimated by performing set of experiments altering catalyst dose from 0.3 g/L to 0.75 g/L at pH 4 and fixed dye concentration 10 mg/L. The photocatalytic degradation rate of ARS dye in the presence of ZnS/CQDsat different doses is shown in Fig. 7(d). It was noticed that on increasing the catalyst amount from 0.3 g/L to 0.5 g/L, the photocatalytic activity of ZnS/CQDs increases from 66.5% to 89%, which might be due to the presence of more number of active sites on the surface of catalyst. Further, an increase in the amount of catalyst dose from 0.5 g/L to 0.75 g/L led to decrease in the photocatalytic degradation of ARS dye to 81.5% which could be attributed to the reduction in penetration of visible light with increase in the turbidity of the solution due to the presence of plenty of substrate molecules, suggesting the optimum catalyst dose for photocatalytic degradation of ARS dye to be 0.5 g/L.

Further, the role of various reactive species was studied by adding different quenchers such as NaN3 (scavenger for singlet oxygen 1O2 and OH), KI (scavenger for h+ and OHsbound to the surface of the catalyst), NaCl (scavenger for h+) and HCOOH (scavenger for e−). Fig. 8 shows the photocatalytic activity of ZnS/CQDs for the degradation of ARS in the presence of different quenchers. About 89% of the dye solution was degraded without the addition of any scavenger due to the participation of active species in the photocatalytic degradation system. Upon addition of 0.01 M of NaN3, HCOOH, NaCl and KI into photocatalytic reaction process, the photocatalytic degradation of ARS decreases from 89% (without scavenger) to 54%, 66%, 61% and 80% respectively. These results suggest that e−, h+, OH and 1O2are the reactive species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of ARS dye.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the enhanced photocatalytic activity of ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic behavior of a photocatalyst depends on several factors such as crystalline nature, light absorption region and efficiency of charge separation and migration. The XRD pattern of ZnS/CQDs is identical to that of cubic phase structure of ZnS, which reveals that crystallinity or crystal structure of ZnS is not accountable for the variation in between the photocatalytic activities of the ZnS and ZnS/CQDs. However, the presence of CQDs is obligatory to achieve the highest degradation rate of ARS dye. Therefore, CQDs play a key role in the improvement of photocatalytic activity.

ZnS/CQDs shows enhanced visible light absorption and narrowing of the bandgap (from 3.07 eV to 3 eV) when compared to ZnS (Fig. S1). This leads to the conclusion that the slight decrease in the bandgap value could be linked to the chemical bonding between ZnS and CQDs, which favours charge migration and can efficiently extend light absorption region to longer wavelengths. CQDscan act as electron acceptor to trap the electrons from ZnS conduction band and thus obstruct electron-hole recombination leaving behind more charge carriers to form reactive species, which can enhance the degradation of organic pollutants. In a photocatalytic mechanism, the quick transfer of charge carriers is crucial to hinder their combinational effect. Infact, PL spectra and UV- DRS studies support this hypothesis. The presence of CQDs contributes to the improved photocatalytic activity of ZnS/CQDsnanocomposite [30].

A diagrammatic representation of the photocatalytic reaction of ZnS/CQDs nanocomposite is displayed in Fig. 9 . When the visible light falls on the ZnS, the electrons get excited from the valence band (VB) of ZnS to the conduction band (CB) of ZnS, leaving behind the holes on the VB. Usually, these photogenerated electrons and holes recombine quickly and then only few charge carriers left which take part in the photocatalytic system. However, upon the introduction of the CQDs to ZnS, the electrons from the CB of the ZnStransfer to CQDs, resulting in an efficient separation of the photo-generated electron and hole pairs. Thence, the photocatalytic performance of the nanocomposite is improved. Ultimately, these photogenerated electrons react with absorbed oxygen forming the superoxide radical anion and the photogenerated holes in the VB of ZnS react with hydroxyl ions in the aqueous solution, generating highly reactive hydroxyl radicals. These hydroxyl radicals will further associate to form hydrogen peroxide, which in turn may react with the superoxide radical anions to reproduce more number of hydroxyl radicals. These highly desirable photo-generated reactive species react with the organic pollutants (ARS), and bring out their photocatalytic degradation, yielding mineralized products [31].
ZnS + hυ → e‾ (CB) + h+ (VB)
e‾ (CB) → e‾ (CQDs)
e‾ (CQDs) + O2 → O2
-
h+ (VB) + H2O → H+ + OH
2 HO → H2O2
H2O2 + O2
- → OH + OH− + O2
Dye + OH/ OH− → CO2 + H2O

ZnS/CQDs photocatalyst was synthesized using a facile chemical precipitation process, which possessed identical crystalline structure of cubic ZnS. The characterization results from various analytical techniques revealed that CQDs were successfully loaded onto the ZnS surface. The introduction of CQDs onto ZnS resulted in the remarkable photocatalytic degradation of ARS dye under visible light irradiation i.e. 89% within 250 min (catalyst dose = 0.5 g/L, pH = 4), as compared to bare ZnS (69%) under similar conditions. The significant increase in photocatalytic performance was assigned to the presence of CQDs. Owing to the photo induced property of CQDs, it improved the photocatalytic activity by enhancing light harvesting, improving interfacial charge transfer, and suppressing charge recombination. In all, this work provides useful information on the design and fabrication of CQDs modified semiconductor materials for remediation of organic pollutants in aqueous media.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors greatly acknowledge the financial support received under the UGC – BSR Government of India through a grant No. F. 25-1/2013(BSR)/5- 91/2007 (BSR) and TEQIP-II grant of Dr. S. S. Bhatnagar UICET, Panjab University, Chandigarh.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.spmi.2016.08.011.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Appl. Phys. A Mater. Sci. Process
